excerpt_en: It's been 4 weeks since I came to Ireland! Every day brings so much to learn, but what's been overwhelmingly the most educational are the meetup events where people from around the world gather. Obviously, the conversations are in English. As much as I can,
body_en:
It's been 4 weeks since I came to Ireland!

Every day brings so much to learn, but

what's been overwhelmingly the most educational are

the meetup events where people from around the world gather.

Obviously, the conversations are in English.

I participate in as many events as possible, and

by mingling with people, I'm forced to speak in English, and

on top of that, I also get to talk about my activities.

Almost every day,

I'm telling different people

about my activities in English, and

when explaining the FOMUS brand,

I'm always thinking about

"how should I present this to get people interested?"

while communicating.

In a way,

this has been incredible training, and

I'm really sharpening my "how to communicate in English" skills.

This is something that

won't improve unless I increase the number of face-to-face interactions,

so I'll keep at it.

One of the most interesting things has been

the events I've been organizing, which I've written about in recent posts.

Right now, I'm running two events:

a card game event and

a walking event, and

the spots for the first sessions filled up in no time!

There are a few reasons for this, and

over these 4 weeks, I've met tons of people and

got them interested in who "MaSU" is.

It's like "If this guy is doing something, I'll join in."

At today's event too,

when I mentioned that I was the organizer

and would be hosting events,

people immediately searched for the group and joined, and

they'll be coming to the walking event

in September!

The content of the events matters, but

"who" is planning and running them

is even more important.

If you want to make something happen overseas,

you first need people to get to know you.

That's also what these events are for.

However, for me,

the success of the events isn't the goal -- it's a means to an end.

To leverage this

and move on to the next stage,

first, over the next 2 weeks,

I'll make these 4 events a success!!

PS

Today at an event, we played UNO.

I got a little jealous of UNO, haha.

I'll work hard so that FOMUS's card game "SILVA"

can become one of the options people reach for!

And,

I had completely finished writing this post, but

actually, after this event,

I also did a Masu Photo shoot!

Today's participant was from Malaysia, and

they had already seen the Malaysia masu photo collection I shot last year, so the shoot went very smoothly, and

their English was very easy to understand.

It was probably because I had been having English conversations for about 4 hours before the shoot that it was extra easy to understand, haha.

title_en: What You Gain Through the Process of Taking on Challenges
excerpt_en: 8/26 After finishing classes, I had 2 Masu Photo shoots today. Ammery, who lives in Dublin and is originally from Russia. She seems to be involved in English-related activities too, so her English was very easy to understand, and communicati
body_en:
8/26

After finishing classes,

I had 2 Masu Photo shoots today.

Ammery, who lives in Dublin

and is originally from Russia.

She seems to be involved in English-related activities too,

so her English was very easy to understand, and

communication went very smoothly.

We shot while walking around Dublin's tourist spots.

It was a fun shoot from start to finish!

She might also come to the walking event I'm planning, so

beyond just the shoot,

being able to build an environment where people can connect

felt really great!

Then, the second shoot was with Minne.

She's someone who wears Lolita fashion.

FOMUS operates with the concept that everyone should be free to express themselves however they want, so

of course, totally OK!

Fashion is different for everyone.

She mentioned she likes anime and such, so

our conversation was lively.

Indeed, when it comes to anime and manga,

if you're communicating with people overseas,

it never hurts to know about them.

I know a fair amount myself, so

it comes in handy at times like this, and

I'm glad I watched all that stuff!

That's how I feel.

Both shoots turned out to be really meaningful!

Scouting the event venue

After that,

I went to scout the venue for

the card game "SILVA"

event scheduled for Thursday.

Finding a place that can fit a lot of people and isn't too noisy

isn't easy, so venue scouting is important.

This time, I happened to find it

while looking for a hotel.

This place is a free space where

as long as you buy a drink,

anyone can hang out.

Plus, there's a second-floor space too,

and that looked great as well!

Seems like lots of possibilities.

I also checked whether reservations were needed, and

they said no,

so as long as we arrive early, it should be fine.

While scouting, I had a Guinness too!

So, while playing card games,

you can have coffee or tea, but

you can also have beer!

That's a great selling point!

So yeah,

even hosting an event requires a lot of preparation.

The first event already has

18 people who've signed up, but

since no one besides me knows the card game and explaining it to too many people is tough, I set the cap at 8.

However, since the number of applicants exceeded expectations and

there were more open seats than I thought, I changed it to 12.

There's a lot to do, but

being able to host events just one month into my Dublin activities might actually be pretty good!

excerpt_en: 8/27 This is a continuation from yesterday's post. This week, I'll be hosting a total of 4 events. 2 card game events. 2 walking events. They're almost fully booked already, so now I need to figure out how to run the events efficiently.
body_en:
8/27

This is a continuation from yesterday's post.

This week, I'll be hosting a total of 4 events.

2 card game events.

2 walking events.

They're almost fully booked already, so

now I need to figure out how to run the events efficiently.

That's what I need to work on.

Today, I want to write about that.

How to share the rules with everyone?

From here, let's talk about the game rules.

I want to explain the rules efficiently, but

explaining everything in English is

still a bit tough for me at this point...

The English version of the rules is already

posted on the official website, so

the basics can be communicated.

But the finer details are hard to get across, so

I wanted to solve this problem somehow!

That's where ChatGPT comes in!

#MySpecialty

I fed ChatGPT

all the information about the card game "SILVA" and

developed a "SILVA RULE GPT"!

https://chatgpt.com/g/g-LssZssUN2-silva-rule-gpt

This way, before asking me directly about the rules,

everyone can look things up on their phones individually.

#AlsoConfiguredToRespondInWhateverLanguageYouAskIn

If I distribute this link

to all participants beforehand,

on the day, they can look up the details on their own, and for anything they still don't understand,

they can just call me over and I'll help.

Whether this will actually work smoothly

won't be clear until we try it at the event, but

by combining the skills we have

to solve problems like this,

new ideas and approaches

that we never would have come up with before start to emerge.

For me, I believe this accumulation of data

will definitely be useful for future activities.

How to make the walking event more efficient?

For the walking event,

Saturday has about 10 people.

Sunday has about 50 people who've shown interest.

I never expected to receive this many offers

for an event I'm hosting in Ireland, but

since people are going to participate, and

moreover, they're paying to participate,

I need to prepare properly.

*5 euros per person, 4 euros for students

My aim is to

create an environment where participants can properly interact with each other. And to create a situation where people can talk with as many others as possible.

Plus, since it's a walking event,

we're walking, and

if 3 or 4 people walk side by side,

it could bother other pedestrians, so

I think the best approach is to have people walk

in 2 rows, in pairs of 2,

and chat.

So I made these cards.

I'll cut each of these out and

hand them to participants on the day.

This way, A1 and A2 chat first,

then B1 and B2 line up together, and so on.

Every 10 minutes,

we switch partners --

the person with "2" moves one position forward,

and the person who was at the very front

goes to the back of the line.

This way, until a full rotation is complete,

everyone is guaranteed to talk to a different person, and

personally, I find one-on-one conversations easier.

And since you're guaranteed to have a conversation,

communication is covered.

Because of the constraints of "walking" and "2 rows max,"

I'm leveraging them to promote conversation.

By the way, once a full rotation is done,

the "1"s go to the front and "2"s go to the back,

and they rotate within their group.

If there's still time after that,

people are free to find their own pairs.

By the way, if someone ends up in a group of 3,

the group of 3 goes to the back of the line.

Then, after walking for about an hour,

we move to a cafe.

You can chat with someone you talked to during the walk, or you can talk to someone you haven't talked to yet,

or you can have a group conversation of three or more.

For that part,

I'm planning to keep it more relaxed and not too rigid.

So that's how it is --

I'm up late every night thinking about how to make the events efficient and run smoothly, and then implementing those ideas.

There's a lot of trial and error involved, but

through trial and error,

I'll keep getting stronger.

title_en: Taking Things One Step at a Time
excerpt_en: Lately, I've been having some really busy days for the first time in a while. That's because work in Japan and overseas is running simultaneously on multiple fronts. On top of that, urgent matters pop up regularly, and I have to deal with those too,
body_en:
Lately,

I've been having some really busy days for the first time in a while.

That's because work in Japan and overseas

is running simultaneously on multiple fronts.

On top of that, urgent matters

pop up regularly, and

I have to deal with those too, so

it's been pretty hectic.

By nature, I really don't like holding the baton (tasks) myself for too long.

To put it simply,

I want to get replies to messages

done and over with as quickly as possible.

Having things sit "unread" bothers me.

But lately,

with managing and planning projects in Japan,

meetings with overseas companies,

and on top of that,

preparing for events in Ireland all piling up,

I don't really like to say this, but

I'm in a situation where "there's just not enough time."

Normally, the Medoc Marathon is coming up, so

I'd want to practice running while drinking wine, but

I haven't been able to do that so far.

That said, complaining about it won't get things done, so I'll tackle each task one at a time, carefully.

At this timing, in Japan,

the "Masu Sharing Service"

is launching.

FOMUS developed specially coated masu, and

we've made them available for rental sharing.

I'm using PRTIMES to announce this, so

I've been writing up the text for that too.

This is something

I haven't been able to delegate to anyone yet, so

I have to do it myself.

On top of that,

creating materials for the walking event and

the card game event,

and printing them, is also part of my workload.

Today I went to a print shop and

printed the papers I need for the events.

Can't do without them, so it was essential.

Additionally, to stay in Ireland for more than 3 months, you have to register as a foreign national, so

I've been preparing the documents for that too.

One way or another, I've been running around non-stop.

The only time I can rest lately

is when I'm eating or sleeping.

Even writing this article is something I'm doing

during the wait time before tonight's meetup event.

*Starts at 7 PM

Meetup events draw a lot of people, so

I dedicate time to the connections I make there.

Spending money and time on connections

is a big investment.

Of course, sometimes it doesn't pan out.

But if I don't attend, the possibility is 0% to begin with.

No matter how busy I am,

I make sure to attend these.

*Especially Wednesday's Japanese-Irish exchange event

So anyway,

my time is up,

so I'm heading off to the event.

If something happens, I'll add more to this post, but

if nothing happens, this is it! Haha

It continues! Haha

I'm writing this on the bus ride home from the exchange event.

To get straight to the point,

I had another interesting encounter today.

A Japanese college student who just arrived in Dublin this week already knew about me.

Of course, I didn't know her.

Why??

Because she had signed up for the event I'm organizing in Ireland.

She happened to find it and

signed up to participate.

So people discover you in ways like this too! Haha

Tomorrow's event is limited to 12 people, so

she's on the waitlist, and on the app,

she can't participate unless someone cancels, but since this kind of encounter makes me happy, and

she's also from Saitama Prefecture so we have a lot in common,

and the conversation was fun,

I decided to invite her specially!

She's only here for 3 weeks, so

if she can have some interesting experiences through the event, that would be amazing!

Also, because I've been attending so many exchange events, the number of people I can say "Hey, long time no see! How's it going?" to has been growing.

This is something you can only get

by showing up again and again, and

I really feel how the grassroots networking pays off.

"Just being there consistently"

is really important, when you think about it!

So anyway,

tomorrow is the first event!

First up is the card game "SILVA" event!!

There are a few concerns, but

I'm going to make sure it's a blast!!

excerpt_en: Well, it's been exactly one month since I came to Ireland. And today was the first event in Ireland! I've been writing about this and that in my previous posts, but this time it was a social event using the card game SILVA.
body_en:
Well, it's been exactly one month since I came to Ireland.

And today was

the first event in Ireland!

I've been writing about this and that in my previous posts, but

this time it was

a social event using the card game SILVA.

Yaoki, a Japanese guy I became friends with at my language school, and I had been talking about the event, so

he agreed to come as support this time.

We arrived a few hours early, and

I explained the rules to him beforehand.

Having even just one other person who knows the rules

makes things much easier.

In terms of numbers, I was planning for

a max of 12, the most I could manage to oversee,

and I expected about half -- 5 people -- to actually show up.

Honestly, since we were recruiting through the app,

to be frank,

I had no idea how many would actually come.

This being a first-time attempt,

I'll use this as a benchmark for future strategy.

And then, the time came.

The one who showed up on time was

Taiki-san, whom I had met at another event a few weeks ago.

And 30 minutes later, he brought his Italian friend along.

Plus, the friend spoke fluent Japanese.

That made 4 of us.

At first, we had planned a social mixer, so

we chatted for about 20 minutes.

Then, since it didn't seem like anyone else was coming,

we started the card game.

By coincidence, everyone could speak Japanese, so

I explained the rules in Japanese as we played.

The quality of the card game's content and rules

is overwhelmingly good, so

once people try it, they enjoy it.

Over about an hour,

I explained the rules while we played.

Everyone caught on quickly, and

they were able to start playing matches right away.

We played 4-player games,

2-player games,

had conversations, and

it was a really meaningful time.

Unfortunately -- or perhaps I should say,

in terms of numbers,

only 2 out of 12 people actually came, so

it was more of a small gathering

than an "event," but

we had some really deep conversations.

Among the 4 of us,

I had the shortest time in Dublin, yet

I was the one organizing the event -- it was a funny situation, but because of that, they recommended some great places to check out.

And then, we decided to go to one of those places on the spot!!

The restaurant is called "Daruma."

It's a Japanese restaurant, and

this was my first Japanese restaurant since coming to Ireland.

Taiki-san connected me with the restaurant's manager.

That said, the owner wasn't there yet.

But surprisingly, the topic of searching for

"masu" came up.

So they showed interest, and

since the owner will apparently be at the restaurant tomorrow,

I decided to go again tomorrow on short notice!!

In Japan,

I've had this kind of experience several times, but

I didn't expect it to happen in Ireland too!

More than wanting to sell masu and make money,

what I'm personally looking for is connections with local people, and

whether "masu sells or doesn't sell"

isn't really the issue.

It's precisely because I've been going to all kinds of events and building connections over this past month that

Taiki-san happened to come today, and

I got introduced through him.

If I hadn't been going to events,

we never would have connected, and

if I hadn't organized my own event,

this opportunity never would have been created.

Moments like these make me glad

I've always been on the move.

The goals of my events are

of course to make them exciting, but also

to make sure participants feel "That was fun" and "I'm glad I came."

And to create connections that

lead to something for FOMUS and my activities --

the number of people at the event doesn't matter.

Because there were only 4 of us,

I think we were able to have deeper conversations.

The app-based event attendance

was honestly below expectations in terms of numbers.

Even so, for me,

there was even just one encounter that

led to something next, so

the event wasn't a failure.

To be honest, this weekend's walking event --

I don't actually know if people will come either.

But unless I actually try,

I won't have any data to make decisions with.

Since I learned this today,

I immediately lifted the restrictions on the waitlist numbers for the walking event, which I had been limiting, and opened it up completely.

We already have about 65 people who've shown interest, so

if even 10 of them come, that would be amazing.

So with that,

I'll keep pushing forward!!

excerpt_en: FOMUS masu, now in Dublin! The first shop is the Japanese restaurant "Daruma." As I wrote in yesterday's post, I connected through an event, got introduced to the restaurant, and the person with decision-making authority was at the shop today, so
body_en:
FOMUS masu, now in Dublin!

The first shop is

the Japanese restaurant "Daruma."

As I wrote in yesterday's post,

I connected through an event,

got introduced to the restaurant, and

the person with decision-making authority

was at the shop today, so

I went for lunch again with Taiki-san, just like yesterday.

I had the daily special katsu set meal!

It had been over 2 months since I'd had a "set meal" at all, so

it was insanely delicious!

After the meal,

I spoke with the manager, Yu-san, and they showed interest in the masu.

Plus, they said they had just been looking for some good masu!

So this time, as a special offer,

I gave them FOMUS's food-grade coated masu --

both a one-go masu and a mini masu.

I'd be happy if they could try them out.

Regular masu tend to deteriorate quickly, but

since ours are coated, that problem is solved.

Having developed masu specifically for restaurants

is a really strong advantage.

And so, one thing led to another,

connections were made, and they agreed to display the masu!

This became the very first location

where FOMUS masu are on display!

Training to run while drinking wine

After the lunch meeting, I headed home once to

get ready for a run.

With only one week left until the Medoc Marathon,

I wanted to practice running while drinking wine.

Plus, I have events this weekend starting tomorrow, so

it's hard to find a big chunk of time.

I put mini red wines in my pocket and

drank them every few kilometers while running!

What I learned was that

the wine didn't taste as good as it normally does.

After all,

wine is best enjoyed sitting at a table.

That said, during practice I'm drinking wine alone, so

it'll probably be different at the actual race, haha.

So anyway, I ran 20 km.

I can run 20 km in a little over 2 hours, so

if I power-walk the remaining 20 km,

there's a chance I might just barely finish.

Well, that said, you never know what'll happen on race day, so I'll keep preparing as best I can.

Tomorrow is the pre-event!

Tomorrow is the first walking event!

Since it's a pre-event,

I was recruiting with a limit of 10, but

I learned from yesterday's event that you can't gauge attendance just from the app's RSVP button, so

I lifted the cap.

Even so, the current count is 12.

I wonder how many will actually come? Haha

Since it's the first time, I won't know anything

until I actually try, so

I just want to collect as much data as possible!

excerpt_en: Today is the last day of August! And today was the pre-event for the walking event! Tomorrow (2024/09/01) was originally the first event, and at the time we had set the capacity at 25 people, but more than double that number ended up on the waitlist,
body_en:
Today is the last day of August!

And

today was the pre-event for the walking event!

Tomorrow (2024/09/01) was originally the first event, and at the time we had set the capacity at 25 people, but

more than double that number

ended up on the waitlist, so

I thought this was going to be a problem and decided to split it up.

I quickly organized a Saturday event too,

making it a limited 10-person event.

The idea was to do a small pre-event

so I could check how I manage things myself.

Being an event organizer

requires the "ability to manage people," and

while I've planned tons of events

in Japan before,

this was my first time overseas.

That's exactly why I wanted to do a practice run first.

So, as "Round 0,"

I held the pre-walking event.

Honestly, after the card game event the other day

where only 2 people showed up from the app,

I was worried that

"what if nobody comes?"

10 minutes before the event time,

I arrived at 8:50 AM, but nobody was there.

Maybe nobody's coming??

I was thinking, but then

I heard someone ask in English, "Is this the event meeting point?"

Someone actually came!

Plus, they said a friend would be coming a bit late!

That made 2!!

Then, more participants kept arriving, and

a total of 7 people joined!

That's a 63% attendance rate!

Even I was surprised!!

So

I got the event started using the strategy I had prepared!

This incorporates strategies I arrived at through extensive trial and error based on many real-life experiences.

This is a key point, so

let me summarize it.

For walking events,

I had participated many times in Japan

as a participant at GPS Runner Shimizu-san's events, and I had already articulated in my mind what was "good and what could be improved."

I copied the good parts, and

for the areas that could be improved,

I improved them before trying them out.

Also, I attended tons of events in Ireland, so I thoroughly copied the good practices of those organizers.

What I combined all of these into

is today's event.

GPS Runner Shimizu-san's events

center around trash picking, so

participants move around freely.

The advantage is that you can talk to anyone, but the downside is that some people end up not being approached.

This creates time where people are just picking up trash,

reducing the communication opportunities for some.

And basically, 2 rows is the max.

*The paths are narrow and spreading out is dangerous

However, I think this 2-row format

is actually really great, because

personally, conversations with 3 or more people are tough for me.

The strength of a walking event is that

I can create an environment where people are forced

to talk one-on-one.

*I can even use "the path is narrow" as a reason

So the two-row strategy was locked in!

Also, keeping track of participant numbers and

confirming payments gets harder

as the crowd grows.

So, when I attended an event in Dublin,

what the organizer was doing was

checking names off on a spreadsheet.

At pub events,

after people paid the participation fee,

they'd enter their name and

be seated -- that was the approach,

and I copied this.

However, in my case,

I had ChatGPT pre-loaded

with the participants' names beforehand and

just pasted them into a spreadsheet.

This way, since the names were already entered ahead of time, I could "shorten the name-entry time."

Additionally, to make sure you can't participate without paying, I added a number card system.

This way, I hand out cards to people who've paid, so

I can tell for certain who's paid and who hasn't.

So I handed out the number cards

to people who paid the participation fee.

Cards with letters and numbers on them.

This strategy was something I came up with on my own, and

it worked perfectly!!

Let's go -- event start!

After collecting payment from everyone,

the event started!

(5 euros for general, 4 euros for students)

*You can participate for under 1,000 yen.

As originally planned,

We started with this formation!!

Since there were fewer people today,

it was easy to explain.

And the walking event began!

By always holding the "A1" number,

I could lead the way at the front.

Then, every 10 minutes, the person with "2" moves one position forward.

This way, everyone gets to

mingle with many people, and

it guarantees one-on-one conversation opportunities.

Since I'm handing out the cards,

participants don't get to choose who they talk to.

It's forced -- that's my event style! Haha

This ensures nobody gets left out of conversations.

And so,

we walked about 3 km in roughly 60 minutes.

We walked along the river, so

the path wasn't too narrow, and

it was easy to manage.

*I discovered this route at a lunch event I attended before

The 10-minute switch worked well, so

everyone just kept talking and talking!! Haha

My plan worked perfectly.

After the walk,

we headed to my go-to Starbucks.

This one has a basement level, and

it's super spacious.

Plus, you don't have to spend money! Haha

After the walk,

everyone just chatted freely. No structure here.

Since they'd already been talking for an hour,

expanding conversations wasn't too difficult.

They could also talk to people they hadn't spoken with yet.

And what surprised me was that

several people asked, "How did you come up with this system?"

The system of "being handed a card"

must have been a first for everyone.

Plus, it's a walking event.

And on top of that,

it fit the event perfectly.

This was something that came from my experience of

attending tons of events on my own two feet,

tuned specifically for this event.

I myself was prepared to improve things

if any errors came up.

*No problems with under 10 people

Reflections on the cafe portion

This time, at the cafe, I had everyone

chat freely without any particular theme.

As the organizer, I get asked lots of questions, so

I was always in conversation mode, but

looking around, some people seemed a bit left out.

Indeed, without one-on-one pairing,

conversations don't start unless there's someone who initiates them.

Of course, if there is someone like that,

it's no problem at all.

So, this is tricky, but

I felt that maybe I should prepare some conversation topic cards for when people run out of things to talk about.

That's probably the only thing I'd improve.

Also, this time everyone understood that there was a participation fee, but

as the number of people grows,

some people might not realize they need to pay.

I want to make sure problems don't arise

when there are more people.

Overall summary

Honestly,

doing this pre-event was the right call.

Suddenly gathering 30+ people would have been

way too hard, I felt. Haha

Tomorrow's event already has

78 people who hit the attend button, and

even if half of them come,

that's about 40 people.

Managing that many is going to be tough. Haha

Plus, the cards use a combination of letters and numbers, so if more than 52 people show up,

there won't be enough numbers, and

the system won't work anymore. Haha

I want to figure this out

by the end of today!

So anyway,

I confirmed that with about 10 people,

this system works.

And I gathered some good data too.

*The lessons I wrote today are my own assets

When the number of people increases,

what kind of troubles will arise?

I won't know until I try, and

I don't know how many people will actually show up either.

Plus, by requiring a participation fee,

I've also created a filter that

makes it harder for troublemakers to join.

Also, to keep running events long-term,

it's tough to do event planning and management as volunteer work.

In fact, becoming an organizer on the app costs over 10,000 yen for six months, and

preparing for events (printing, building systems) takes my own time and money too.

Getting people to understand this

is quite difficult, but

money is necessary to keep events going.

This time, 7 people participated, so

a total of 33 euros was collected.

*2 students

That's about 5,300 yen.

Of course, this money will go toward

event operating costs.

First, I'll recoup the money I paid

to become an organizer on the app.

By building a solid business model,

my event management becomes more stable, and

I can create even higher-quality events.

I'll make this work as a proper business and

create events that last for a long time!!

title_en: Benefits of Hosting International Exchange Events Overseas
excerpt_en: 9/1 A whirlwind of events! Since it's Sunday, I had a walking event starting at 9 AM, and a card game event in the afternoon. #BothEventsOrganizedByMe For the walking event, we had over 70 sign-ups, so I was wondering how it would go,
body_en:
9/1

A whirlwind of events!

Since it's Sunday,

I had a walking event starting at 9 AM,

and a card game event in the afternoon.

#BothEventsOrganizedByMe

For the walking event,

we had over 70 sign-ups, and

I was wondering how it would go, but

10 people actually came!

All of them found us through the app.

One of them

also participated in yesterday's event.

Having someone come two days in a row

is truly something to be grateful for.

The numbers might seem small, but

personally, it's a manageable size where I can interact with everyone, and

if you want to build strong connections,

about 10 people feels just right!

Since I had done the pre-event yesterday,

I improved the logistics even further, and

everything went very smoothly.

"Walk 3 km in about 60 minutes while having international exchanges" --

it's a simple event, but

the excuse of "the path is narrow so we walk in 2 rows" allows me to

guarantee one-on-one interactions, which

was very well-received by the participants!

Explaining a card game in English

Then, in the afternoon, I held the card game event!

Of course, we played "SILVA,"

which was created in the FOMUS community last year.

6 people (all meeting for the first time) joined this event!

Since nobody besides me knew the rules,

I explained them in English!

At first, they struggled to learn, but

with the English version of the official website I had prepared and

the GPT specialized for rule explanations,

we were able to clear most issues.

For anything they really couldn't figure out,

the solution was "call MaSU over"!

With this approach, in about 30 minutes,

everyone understood the rules, and

in the end,

they were able to play

without me explaining anything.

Once again, the card game that

the FOMUS community poured so much into --

from design to test plays --

crossing the ocean and being played as a communication tool at international exchange events is truly moving.

#HugeThanksToTheFOMUSCommunity

Plus, within the rules of "SILVA,"

"using a FOMUS masu gives you a powerful effect," so

even people who had never heard of masu (everyone)

found themselves "somehow excited to use the masu."

Haha

Watching everyone happily play the "FOMUS masu" card

on the field,

it made me happy to see the concept of "connecting through masu" come to life.

Increasing the number of encounters

I held both the walking and card game events

on the same day at different times, and

connections were forming at each one.

Seeing horizontal networking happen

was really great, and

it made hosting them worthwhile.

Of course, for me too,

actually running events overseas

taught me so much, and

people got to know the FOMUS brand, and

I built a monetization model to sustain the events.

And then, at the card game event,

someone arrived late, and

I was explaining the rules and playing one-on-one with them. At the end, when we talked about work,

it turned out they had been a journalist

in Ireland for many years, and

they said, "What FOMUS is doing with these events is interesting -- I might cover it sometime."

At international exchange events overseas,

people from all sorts of backgrounds participate, so

there's a chance to meet people you'd never normally connect with. If I can establish myself as the organizer in that space, I can differentiate myself overwhelmingly from other event hosts, so

I'm going to go after this seriously!

excerpt_en: 9/2 After finishing the first half of classes, I slipped out during break time to do my foreign national registration in Ireland. This is something everyone staying in Dublin for more than 3 months must do, and it was time to apply. The venue
body_en:
9/2

After finishing the first half of classes,

I slipped out during break time to

do my foreign national registration in Ireland.

This is something everyone staying in Dublin for more than 3 months must do, and

it was time to apply.

The venue was about a 10-minute walk from school, so

it was relatively close.

https://www.notion.so

https://www.notion.so

This is how the reception looked, and

the procedure was done in person.

The questions were simple, so I breezed through it.

It took about an hour, and

I was about to head back to class, but

the building had been locked and I couldn't get in...

I mean, with only 20 minutes of class left,

I guess it makes sense they wouldn't let a student in at that point.

Unfortunately, I couldn't attend class.

But with that, my foreign national registration is complete, so

all I have to do now is wait about 2 weeks for the certificate to arrive.

I need to have it delivered to my homestay address, so it looks like I'll just make it in time.

*I don't fully trust the next place

Off to the library

Dublin has lots of libraries.

I hadn't properly visited one yet, so

I decided to check one out.

The library was clean and easy to study in, and

I could do computer work there without any issues.

However, I was so exhausted from recently that

I fell asleep at the desk, and

sure enough, someone woke me up. Haha

So I went home right away. Haha

At home, I handled shipping the America photo collection and

sending out the FOMUS uniforms, which were

crowdfunding return gifts.

The timing of shipping the uniforms was to

coincide with the Medoc Marathon.

I was able to deliver them at the perfect timing.

excerpt_en: This article doesn't have much in the way of lessons, so feel free to skip it. Something happened that annoyed me a little. It was at the language school. A lot of students charge their phones and stuff there, and I had plugged in my charger too.
body_en:
This article doesn't have much in the way of lessons, so

feel free to skip it.

Something happened that annoyed me a little.

It was at the language school.

A lot of students charge their phones and stuff there, and I had plugged in my charger to charge it too.

During a class break,

a Mongolian woman walked behind me, and

I heard a "CRACK" sound.

My power adapter had been

snapped right off at the base.

Plus, the broken piece of my plug

was still stuck in the socket.

The Mongolian woman

was big, and

the walkway was narrow, so

she destroyed everything in her path.

Even though it wasn't intentional,

honestly, having something that directly impacts my work destroyed

was a big shock, and

the "sorry, bye" vibe was strong, so

I got annoyed for the first time in a while.

Plus, since it's a Japanese-to-international plug converter, it's not easy to find one locally.

Beyond just the money,

having something destroyed that you can't buy in Japan

was brutal.

The destroyed plug

Timing

But what saved me at this timing was that

the Medoc Marathon is coming up this week, and

two members from Japan will be joining me.

So they could bring me a new plug!

What great timing!

The expense hurts, but

not having a charging device

would seriously tank my productivity, so

I had no choice but to buy one.

For now,

it looks like I can get the plug this week.

The bad feelings inside me

Maybe I shouldn't write this, but

I want to note that this part of me still exists.

This is my immature side.

If a different girl -- say, a Turkish classmate --

had broken the plug,

I wouldn't have been this irritated.

It just happened to be someone else.

She didn't smile much, and

she was someone I personally wasn't fond of, and the fact that she didn't even properly apologize

made it worse.

Honestly, if it had been someone I found cute,

I probably wouldn't have been annoyed.

*I'd still be shocked, though.

In the end, even at 29 years old,

I still judge people by their looks to some extent.

I'm nicer to people I find attractive, and

I'm not interested in the rest --

that tendency really stood out.

I can't change this instinct, so

I'll work on controlling these feelings better.

*What am I even talking about
